candidate_problems.text: How can the vague systems-level notion of 'feedback-fertile boundary conditions' be made precise enough to distinguish genuinely generative initial conditions from merely low-entropy ones? The conjecture shifts the explanandum from improbable microstate to global interaction architecture, but it lacks a quantitative criterion for 'dynamical accessibility,' 'usable gradients,' 'memory,' or 'open-ended structure formation.' A key open problem is to define measurable order parameters or invariants over law-plus-boundary-condition pairs that predict whether multiscale feedback and knowledge-bearing structures are reachable.

What explains the origin or naturalness of the special law-plus-boundary-condition combination itself, without reintroducing teleology or merely renaming the fine-tuning problem? The conjecture usefully relocates specialness to a systems-level boundary condition, but this may only redescribe the mystery unless there is some deeper meta-law, measure over possible universes, or consistency principle that makes such fertile beginnings expected. The unresolved tension is whether the proposal is explanatory progress or an elegant reformulation of why the universe began in a rare complexity-permitting regime at all.

Is there a robust causal link between cosmological feedback structure and the emergence of knowledge-growth specifically, rather than complexity in a looser sense? The conjecture connects early-universe dynamics to error-correcting, memory-bearing, selectively retained structures, but it remains unclear which additional conditions are required for open-ended epistemic systems rather than transient complex chemistry or local self-organization. A worthwhile problem is to identify what structural features separate universes that produce durable knowledge-creating agents from those that merely produce intricate but non-cumulative complexity.
